title: test: podman system service doesn't leak mount on termination
body:
#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
Now "podman system service" does not leak a mount when it is terminated
```

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *giuseppe* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [giuseppe] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

> Then on normal cleanup c/storage unmounts the path again. This doesn't make any sense to me for podman because it cannot ever work when podman commands are run in parallel. As such we cannot ever assume the path is actually private.
> 
> So what is the goal of the private mount? If we actually want the path private then we must leak it and cannot unmount it at the end of the process. Otherwise if we do not actually need it private we should really skip the private mount IMO.

that affects only the home directory for the storage (`$STORE/$DRIVER`), e.g. for root and overlay `/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay`.

That mount is created only while the driver holds a lock, otherwise there is the risk that multiple of such mounts could be created, hiding previous mounts below its path (it happened in the past).

> > Then on normal cleanup c/storage unmounts the path again. This doesn't make any sense to me for podman because it cannot ever work when podman commands are run in parallel. As such we cannot ever assume the path is actually private.
> > So what is the goal of the private mount? If we actually want the path private then we must leak it and cannot unmount it at the end of the process. Otherwise if we do not actually need it private we should really skip the private mount IMO.
> 
> that affects only the home directory for the storage (`$STORE/$DRIVER`), e.g. for root and overlay `/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay`.
> 
> That mount is created only while the driver holds a lock, otherwise there is the risk that multiple of such mounts could be created, hiding previous mounts below its path (it happened in the past).

@giuseppe What I meant is that the race is between two podman process setting up and cleanup the store. The lock doesn't prevent anything for this case

In one terminal run
```
sudo bin/podman system service -t0
```

then in another
```
$ sudo findmnt -o TARGET,PROPAGATION /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay
TARGET                              PROPAGATION
/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay private
$ sudo podman ps
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE       COMMAND     CREATED     STATUS      PORTS       NAMES
$ sudo findmnt -o TARGET,PROPAGATION /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay
<--- exit code 1 error mount is not private because the ps command did an unmount in cleanup.
```

This means that from now on the system service sees a shared store. Hence my question what the goal of make private is?

this is something we always had in storage and I think it was added to prevent that mounts on top of it can leak into other containers. It has also some performance benefits as the kernel (at least older versions, not sure now) doesn't have to clone all the mounts to then unmount them when there are many containers.
the mount was cleaned since there are no containers running/mounted. It will be recreated when needed, and we have locking in place for that
